title: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)
oneLiner: Common progressive enlargement of the prostate in older men causing lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS); can lead to acute urinary retention (AUR) and renal impairment if untreated.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Urinary sepsis (urosepsis):** fever, rigors, hypotension, flank pain + raised lactate — immediate catheterisation, blood cultures, IV antibiotics *before* any other workup.
- **Post-renal acute kidney injury:** anuria, palpable bladder, rising creatinine — urgent bladder decompression with catheter (suprapubic if urethral impossible).
- **Prostate cancer with bladder outlet obstruction:** hard, irregular DRE (nodules, asymmetry) + raised PSA — needs urgent urology referral (not same-hour emergency but must not be missed).
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (LUTS):**
  - **Storage:** frequency, urgency, nocturia, urge incontinence
  - **Voiding:** hesitancy, poor stream, intermittency, straining, terminal dribbling
  - **Post-void:** feeling of incomplete emptying
- **Complications:** acute urinary retention (painful inability to void), chronic retention (overflow incontinence, recurrent UTIs), haematuria
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **DRE:** smooth, enlarged, symmetrical prostate (normal: walnut; BPH: moderate enlargement); note any hard nodules, asymmetry, or loss of sulcus → suspect malignancy
  - **Bladder palpation / percussion:** distended bladder on suprapubic exam or percussion dullness (chronic retention)
  - **Neuro exam:** check anal tone, perineal sensation, lower limb reflexes (to exclude cauda equina)
  - **General:** assess for fluid overload if AKI present
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Prostate cancer:** hard DRE, raised PSA, asymmetrical gland, often asymptomatic early but can cause obstruction
- **Urinary tract infection (prostatitis):** painful DRE, fever, perineal pain, urine dip positive for nitrites/leucocytes
- **Neurogenic bladder (e.g., spinal cord compression, diabetes):** poor anal tone, saddle anaesthesia, high post-void residual without gland enlargement
- **Urethral stricture:** history of instrumentation/STI, poor stream + straining, but DRE normal, post-void residual variable
- **Overactive bladder (OAB):** urgency and frequency without obstructive DRE or high post-void residual; no hesitancy
- **Detrusor underactivity:** large post-void residual + weak stream, but DRE normal, BPH may be absent
- ****Splitter:** DRE + post-void residual: hard prostate → suspect cancer; high residual but normal DRE → neurogenic or detrusor underactivity**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick: exclude UTI (nitrites, leucocytes, blood)
  - Bladder scanner (post-void residual): >100ml indicates incomplete emptying; >300ml suggests chronic retention
  - Flow rate (if available): low Qmax (<10mL/s, typical BPH)
- **Bloods:**
  - **U&E + creatinine** (mandatory – assess renal function)
  - PSA (controversial – only if suspect cancer, discuss with patient; avoid if UTI present)
  - FBC (rule out infection)
  - BMP (if retention – check for hyperkalaemia in AKI)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** USS renal tract (to assess hydronephrosis, post-void residual, and kidney size)
  - **Second-line:** CT IVU or MRI prostate (if suspicious of cancer or complex obstruction)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urodynamics (if mixed symptoms or inconclusive)
  - Cystoscopy (if haematuria, suspected stricture, or prior to surgery)
- **Scoring:** **IPSS (International Prostate Symptom Score)** – stratify severity: mild (0–7), moderate (8–19), severe (20–35). Score >20 usually needs urology referral.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Post-void residual bladder scan** – omitted entirely, you will miss chronic retention and risk renal failure.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute retention: **catheterise** (16–18Fr Foley, fill balloon with 10ml water; if difficulty, try larger catheter or consider suprapubic – obtain consent)
  - Start **alpha-blocker** (tamsulosin 400mcg PO OD) to aid passage of catheter and improve voiding post-trial
  - If urosepsis: IV antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or cefuroxime 1.5g IV TDS) after cultures
  - Give analgesia (paracetamol or NSAIDs if no contraindication)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor fluid balance, urine output, and renal function
  - **Trial Without Catheter (TWOC)** after 24–72h if no contraindication (if successful, patient likely to void; if fails, needs ongoing catheter and urology referral)
  - Start **5-alpha-reductase inhibitor** (e.g., finasteride 5mg OD) if prostate >30g, to reduce size for long-term control (takes 3–6 months)
  - Consider combination therapy (alpha-blocker + 5ARI) for moderate-severe BPH
  - If haematuria: ensure catheter drainage, increase fluid intake; if heavy, urology consult for irrigation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: reduce caffeine/alcohol, timed voiding, double-voiding
  - Medical therapy: continue alpha-blocker ± 5ARI (review every 6–12 months)
  - Surgical options if medical failure: **TURP** (transurethral resection of prostate), laser enucleation, or open prostatectomy (very large glands)
  - PSA monitoring only if cancer risk (shared decision)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 80–100mL/hr if AKI or NBM; otherwise oral. VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (unless bleeding). Diet: NBM if planned for EUA/TURP; otherwise free. Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, urine output chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **Alpha-blocker:** Tamsulosin 400mcg PO OD (start immediately for acute symptoms)
  - **5-alpha-reductase inhibitor:** Finasteride 5mg PO OD (for prostate >30g; takes months)
  - **Alternative alpha-blocker:** Alfuzosin MR 10mg PO OD, doxazosin 4–8mg PO OD (titrate, monitor BP)
  - **Anticholinergic (if overactive component):** Solifenacin 5mg PO OD (use with caution; can worsen voiding)
- **Key interactions:**
  - Alpha-blockers + PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) → severe hypotension (avoid combination)
  - 5ARIs + anticoagulants → increased haematuria risk
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Tamsulosin – no change. Finasteride – caution in hepatic impairment. All 5ARIs – avoid in severe hepatic failure.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing an alpha-blocker to a patient with a history of orthostatic hypotension or on high-dose diuretics without checking lying/standing BP → risk of syncope and fall.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Acute urinary retention (AUR)
  - Urosepsis (from infected urine post-obstruction)
  - Haematuria (often from prostatic veins)
  - Catheter-associated UTI
- **Chronic:**
  - Bladder trabeculation, diverticula, stones
  - Hydronephrosis, renal failure (post-renal)
  - Recurrent UTIs
  - Overflow incontinence
sections.Prognosis: - BPH is progressive but treatable: moderate symptoms can improve with medical therapy (alpha-blocker + 5ARI). Surgical resection (TURP) offers >80% success in symptom relief. Chronic retention with renal impairment may not fully recover even with decompression. Untreated, risk of renal failure and hospitalisation for AUR increases.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a DRE** before ordering PSA – if hard/irregular, delay PSA until after urology consult (false positive from prostatic manipulation is minor; but clinical suspicion overrides).
- **If catheterising for retention**, drain slowly (first 500-1000mL immediately, then clamp for 1 hour if concerned about post-obstructive diuresis) – monitor urine output closely.
- **In an older man with new LUTS, always check renal function** – elevated creatinine may be the only sign of chronic retention.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Avoid starting a 5ARI (finasteride/dutasteride) without an alpha-blocker first** – can worsen acute retention in the first 3–6 months.
- **Do not treat a patient with LUTS and a high PSA without discussing cancer risk** – shared decision-making is essential.
- **Never delay catheterisation in a patient with anuria and palpable bladder** awaiting USS – the USS can wait; decompression is the priority.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to pass urine for >8 hours (or has catheter: catheter blocked, no urine output in 6 hours)
  - Fever, rigors, or new flank pain (urosepsis)
  - Sudden worsening of symptoms or heavy haematuria with clots
- **Follow-up:**
  - If started on alpha-blocker – review in 4–6 weeks (assess BP, symptom score)
  - If on 5ARI – review at 6 months for effect; PSA check (level drops 50% after 6 months – if not, suspect malignancy)
  - If failed TWOC – refer to urology for further evaluation (outpatient or clinic within 2 weeks)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / urology registrar if:**
  - Urosepsis with hypotension (MAP <65mmHg) despite fluids and antibiotics
  - Anuria after catheter placement (check position; if in bladder and no output → suspect obstruction above – call UROLOGY)
  - Acute renal failure with K+ >6.0 or creatinine rising rapidly
  - Haematuria with clots causing catheter blockage
- **Call ICU / Medical Emergency Team if:**
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - GCS <13 or any acute deterioration
  - Anuric with refractory hyperkalaemia despite medical management

rawMarkdown:
## Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)
**One-liner:** Common progressive enlargement of the prostate in older men causing lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS); can lead to acute urinary retention (AUR) and renal impairment if untreated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Urinary sepsis (urosepsis):** fever, rigors, hypotension, flank pain + raised lactate — immediate catheterisation, blood cultures, IV antibiotics *before* any other workup.
- **Post-renal acute kidney injury:** anuria, palpable bladder, rising creatinine — urgent bladder decompression with catheter (suprapubic if urethral impossible).
- **Prostate cancer with bladder outlet obstruction:** hard, irregular DRE (nodules, asymmetry) + raised PSA — needs urgent urology referral (not same-hour emergency but must not be missed).

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (LUTS):**
  - **Storage:** frequency, urgency, nocturia, urge incontinence
  - **Voiding:** hesitancy, poor stream, intermittency, straining, terminal dribbling
  - **Post-void:** feeling of incomplete emptying
- **Complications:** acute urinary retention (painful inability to void), chronic retention (overflow incontinence, recurrent UTIs), haematuria
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **DRE:** smooth, enlarged, symmetrical prostate (normal: walnut; BPH: moderate enlargement); note any hard nodules, asymmetry, or loss of sulcus → suspect malignancy
  - **Bladder palpation / percussion:** distended bladder on suprapubic exam or percussion dullness (chronic retention)
  - **Neuro exam:** check anal tone, perineal sensation, lower limb reflexes (to exclude cauda equina)
  - **General:** assess for fluid overload if AKI present

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Prostate cancer:** hard DRE, raised PSA, asymmetrical gland, often asymptomatic early but can cause obstruction
- **Urinary tract infection (prostatitis):** painful DRE, fever, perineal pain, urine dip positive for nitrites/leucocytes
- **Neurogenic bladder (e.g., spinal cord compression, diabetes):** poor anal tone, saddle anaesthesia, high post-void residual without gland enlargement
- **Urethral stricture:** history of instrumentation/STI, poor stream + straining, but DRE normal, post-void residual variable
- **Overactive bladder (OAB):** urgency and frequency without obstructive DRE or high post-void residual; no hesitancy
- **Detrusor underactivity:** large post-void residual + weak stream, but DRE normal, BPH may be absent
- ****Splitter:** DRE + post-void residual: hard prostate → suspect cancer; high residual but normal DRE → neurogenic or detrusor underactivity**

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick: exclude UTI (nitrites, leucocytes, blood)
  - Bladder scanner (post-void residual): >100ml indicates incomplete emptying; >300ml suggests chronic retention
  - Flow rate (if available): low Qmax (<10mL/s, typical BPH)
- **Bloods:**
  - **U&E + creatinine** (mandatory – assess renal function)
  - PSA (controversial – only if suspect cancer, discuss with patient; avoid if UTI present)
  - FBC (rule out infection)
  - BMP (if retention – check for hyperkalaemia in AKI)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** USS renal tract (to assess hydronephrosis, post-void residual, and kidney size)
  - **Second-line:** CT IVU or MRI prostate (if suspicious of cancer or complex obstruction)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urodynamics (if mixed symptoms or inconclusive)
  - Cystoscopy (if haematuria, suspected stricture, or prior to surgery)
- **Scoring:** **IPSS (International Prostate Symptom Score)** – stratify severity: mild (0–7), moderate (8–19), severe (20–35). Score >20 usually needs urology referral.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Post-void residual bladder scan** – omitted entirely, you will miss chronic retention and risk renal failure.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute retention: **catheterise** (16–18Fr Foley, fill balloon with 10ml water; if difficulty, try larger catheter or consider suprapubic – obtain consent)
  - Start **alpha-blocker** (tamsulosin 400mcg PO OD) to aid passage of catheter and improve voiding post-trial
  - If urosepsis: IV antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or cefuroxime 1.5g IV TDS) after cultures
  - Give analgesia (paracetamol or NSAIDs if no contraindication)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor fluid balance, urine output, and renal function
  - **Trial Without Catheter (TWOC)** after 24–72h if no contraindication (if successful, patient likely to void; if fails, needs ongoing catheter and urology referral)
  - Start **5-alpha-reductase inhibitor** (e.g., finasteride 5mg OD) if prostate >30g, to reduce size for long-term control (takes 3–6 months)
  - Consider combination therapy (alpha-blocker + 5ARI) for moderate-severe BPH
  - If haematuria: ensure catheter drainage, increase fluid intake; if heavy, urology consult for irrigation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: reduce caffeine/alcohol, timed voiding, double-voiding
  - Medical therapy: continue alpha-blocker ± 5ARI (review every 6–12 months)
  - Surgical options if medical failure: **TURP** (transurethral resection of prostate), laser enucleation, or open prostatectomy (very large glands)
  - PSA monitoring only if cancer risk (shared decision)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 80–100mL/hr if AKI or NBM; otherwise oral. VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (unless bleeding). Diet: NBM if planned for EUA/TURP; otherwise free. Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, urine output chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **Alpha-blocker:** Tamsulosin 400mcg PO OD (start immediately for acute symptoms)
  - **5-alpha-reductase inhibitor:** Finasteride 5mg PO OD (for prostate >30g; takes months)
  - **Alternative alpha-blocker:** Alfuzosin MR 10mg PO OD, doxazosin 4–8mg PO OD (titrate, monitor BP)
  - **Anticholinergic (if overactive component):** Solifenacin 5mg PO OD (use with caution; can worsen voiding)
- **Key interactions:**
  - Alpha-blockers + PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) → severe hypotension (avoid combination)
  - 5ARIs + anticoagulants → increased haematuria risk
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Tamsulosin – no change. Finasteride – caution in hepatic impairment. All 5ARIs – avoid in severe hepatic failure.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing an alpha-blocker to a patient with a history of orthostatic hypotension or on high-dose diuretics without checking lying/standing BP → risk of syncope and fall.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Acute urinary retention (AUR)
  - Urosepsis (from infected urine post-obstruction)
  - Haematuria (often from prostatic veins)
  - Catheter-associated UTI
- **Chronic:**
  - Bladder trabeculation, diverticula, stones
  - Hydronephrosis, renal failure (post-renal)
  - Recurrent UTIs
  - Overflow incontinence

### Prognosis
- BPH is progressive but treatable: moderate symptoms can improve with medical therapy (alpha-blocker + 5ARI). Surgical resection (TURP) offers >80% success in symptom relief. Chronic retention with renal impairment may not fully recover even with decompression. Untreated, risk of renal failure and hospitalisation for AUR increases.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a DRE** before ordering PSA – if hard/irregular, delay PSA until after urology consult (false positive from prostatic manipulation is minor; but clinical suspicion overrides).
- **If catheterising for retention**, drain slowly (first 500-1000mL immediately, then clamp for 1 hour if concerned about post-obstructive diuresis) – monitor urine output closely.
- **In an older man with new LUTS, always check renal function** – elevated creatinine may be the only sign of chronic retention.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Avoid starting a 5ARI (finasteride/dutasteride) without an alpha-blocker first** – can worsen acute retention in the first 3–6 months.
- **Do not treat a patient with LUTS and a high PSA without discussing cancer risk** – shared decision-making is essential.
- **Never delay catheterisation in a patient with anuria and palpable bladder** awaiting USS – the USS can wait; decompression is the priority.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to pass urine for >8 hours (or has catheter: catheter blocked, no urine output in 6 hours)
  - Fever, rigors, or new flank pain (urosepsis)
  - Sudden worsening of symptoms or heavy haematuria with clots
- **Follow-up:**
  - If started on alpha-blocker – review in 4–6 weeks (assess BP, symptom score)
  - If on 5ARI – review at 6 months for effect; PSA check (level drops 50% after 6 months – if not, suspect malignancy)
  - If failed TWOC – refer to urology for further evaluation (outpatient or clinic within 2 weeks)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / urology registrar if:**
  - Urosepsis with hypotension (MAP <65mmHg) despite fluids and antibiotics
  - Anuria after catheter placement (check position; if in bladder and no output → suspect obstruction above – call UROLOGY)
  - Acute renal failure with K+ >6.0 or creatinine rising rapidly
  - Haematuria with clots causing catheter blockage
- **Call ICU / Medical Emergency Team if:**
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - GCS <13 or any acute deterioration
  - Anuric with refractory hyperkalaemia despite medical management
